prs.title: DOC: add practical mcp client setup guide
prs.body:
this adds a practical setup guide for using sktime-mcp with real mcp clients and local agent projects.

right now the repo has bits of setup information in a few places, but not one page that someone can follow when they are trying to connect claude desktop, cursor, vscode style clients, or a custom local agent.

this pr adds a new client setup page and links it from the getting started docs.

it covers:
- install and run options
- claude desktop config
- cursor or vscode style config
- custom local agent setup
- a quick sanity check workflow
- common setup mistakes and how to fix them

i also checked that the docs build still succeeds locally.

prs.title: DOC/TEST: add external agent integration walkthrough
prs.body:
## Summary
Closes #419.

This PR adds a small but concrete workflow artifact for integrating `sktime-mcp` from an external agent codebase.

## What changed
- add a new runnable example: `examples/08_external_agent_integration.py`
- add a smoke test for that example
- extend the user guide with:
  - editable-install guidance for external agent projects
  - a `PYTHONPATH` fallback for temporary source-tree integrations
  - a concrete `evaluate_estimator` usage example with the current MCP contract
  - guidance to inspect returned handle metadata before model selection

## Why this matters
This contribution maps directly to real MCP adoption work:
- it helps users wire `sktime-mcp` into their own agent projects
- it makes setup less error-prone
- it gives agents and developers a tested example for combining custom-data loading, forecasting, and evaluation

## Validation

prs.title: FEAT: expose explicit data shape metadata in load_data_source
prs.body:
## Summary
Closes #416.

This adds explicit agent-friendly shape metadata to `load_data_source` and `load_data_source_async` responses so MCP clients can reason about loaded data handles without guessing from raw column names and dtypes.

## What changed
- add a shared metadata builder in `Executor`
- expose:
  - `target_scitype`
  - `target_variates`
  - `has_exog`
  - `exog_variates`
  - `index_type`
  - `n_target_columns`
  - `n_exog_columns`
- apply the same metadata enrichment to both sync and async load paths
- add focused tests for:
  - datetime-indexed target with multivariate exogenous data
  - range-indexed target without exogenous data

## Why this matters
This makes loaded data self-describing for agents before they choose estimators or workflow branches. In particular, it reduces ambiguity around:
- whether a handle is univariate or multivariate
- whether exogenous variables are present
- whether the time index is datetime-like or integer/range based

## Validation
- `.venv/bin/ruff check src/sktime_mcp/runtime/executor.py tests/test_data_sources.py`
- `.venv/bin/ruff format --check src/sktime_mcp/runtime/executor.py tests/test_data_sources.py`

prs.title: FIX: support RangeIndex inputs in auto-formatting
prs.body:
## Summary
This fixes an internal auto-format failure for valid integer-indexed / `RangeIndex` time series.

Closes #390.

## Problem
`load_data_source` accepts integer-indexed pandas data, but the auto-format path assumes every index exposes datetime-like frequency metadata. With `RangeIndex`, that raised an internal `AttributeError` on `index.freq`, logged a warning, and fell back to the unformatted path.

## Changes
- guard frequency inspection so it only runs for datetime / period indexes
- preserve the existing `Integer` frequency metadata for non-datetime indexes
- add a regression test covering the integer-indexed pandas load path with auto-format enabled

## Verification

prs.title: FIX: reject non-forecasting demo datasets in load_dataset
prs.body:
## Summary
- detect auto-discovered supervised demo datasets returned as `(X, y)`
- fail fast with a clear error instead of surfacing class labels as forecasting `exog`
- add focused regression coverage for supported forecasting demos and rejected non-forecasting demos

## Why
`Executor.load_dataset()` currently assumes tuple-returning demo loaders use forecasting semantics `(y, X)`. Because demo loaders are auto-discovered from all `sktime.datasets.load_*` functions, non-forecasting datasets like `basic_motions` are also exposed. prs.title: FIX: validate cancel_job delete flag type
prs.body:
Closes #359

## Summary
- validate the `delete` flag type in `cancel_job_tool`
- reject non-boolean values before any job record is removed
- add a regression test for `delete="false"`

## Problem
`cancel_job_tool` treats the `delete` parameter as a plain truthy/falsy value.
That means malformed MCP calls like:

```python
cancel_job_tool(job_id, delete="false")
```

still delete job records, because non-empty strings are truthy in Python.

I reproduced this on current code by creating a completed job and calling `cancel_job_tool(job_id, delete="false")`, which returned success and removed the job.

## Fix
Add a strict boolean validation guard near the start of `cancel_job_tool`:
- if `delete` is not a real `bool`, return a validation error
- do not delete anything in that case

## Tests
Added regression coverage to ensure:
- `cancel_job_tool(job_id, delete="false")` returns an error
- the job record remains present after the invalid call

## Verification
- `ruff check .`
- `ruff format --check .`
- `pytest -q`

prs.title: FIX: reject boolean pagination values in discovery and job tools
prs.body:
Closes #356

## Summary
- reject boolean `limit` values in `search_estimators_tool`
- reject boolean `limit` and `offset` values in `list_estimators_tool`
- reject boolean `limit` values in `list_jobs_tool`
- add focused regression tests for the bool cases

## Problem
These tools validated pagination parameters with numeric comparisons like `limit < 1`, which lets Python boolean values through because `bool` is a subclass of `int`.

Before this change:

```python
search_estimators_tool("NaiveForecaster", limit=True)
# success=True, behaves like limit=1

list_estimators_tool(limit=True)
# success=True, returns one estimator and echoes "limit": True

list_estimators_tool(limit=2, offset=True)
# success=True, behaves like offset=1

list_jobs_tool(limit=True)
# success=True
```

For MCP/JSON tools, `true`/`false` should not silently become pagination values.

## Fix
Use strict integer validation for pagination parameters by rejecting `bool` before the existing range checks.

## Tests
Added regression coverage for:
- `search_estimators_tool(..., limit=True)`
- `list_estimators_tool(limit=True)`
- `list_estimators_tool(limit=2, offset=True)`
- `list_jobs_tool(limit=True)`

## Verification
- `ruff check .`
- `ruff format --check .`
- `pytest -q`

prs.title: FIX: reject boolean horizon values in forecasting tools
prs.body:
Closes #348

## Summary
- reject `True`/`False` in the shared horizon validator used by forecasting tools
- keep error messaging consistent with other non-integer horizon inputs
- add focused regression tests for sync, async, and predict entrypoints

## Problem
`fit_predict_tool` and `fit_predict_async_tool` accepted boolean horizon values because Python treats `bool` as a subclass of `int`.

Before this change:

```python
fit_predict_tool(handle, "airline", horizon=True)
```

returned success and behaved like a 1-step forecast.

Similarly:

```python
fit_predict_async_tool(handle, dataset="airline", horizon=True)
```

scheduled a background job with `horizon=True`.

For MCP/JSON tool use, that is a bad contract: `true`/`false` should not silently become valid forecast horizons.

## Fix
The shared `_validate_horizon()` helper now treats `bool` as invalid, so all forecasting entrypoints reject it consistently.

## Tests
Added regression coverage for:
- `predict_tool(..., horizon=True)`
- `predict_tool(..., horizon=False)`
- `fit_predict_tool(..., horizon=True)`
- `fit_predict_async_tool(..., horizon=True)`

## Verification
- `ruff check .`
- `ruff format --check .`
- `pytest -q`

prs.title: FIX: retain active job records when delete=true is requested
prs.body:
## Reference Issues/PRs

Closes #166.

## What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.

This PR fixes the current equivalent of the old `delete_job()` ghost-deletion bug described in #166.

The original issue predates the tool consolidation, but the same behavior still exists today through `cancel_job(job_id, delete=True)`:

- if a job is `pending` or `running`, the tool marks it cancelled
- then immediately deletes the job record
- but the background coroutine can continue running and reporting late updates

That leaves MCP clients with no way to inspect a still-active task and makes cancellation semantics misleading.

This PR makes two focused changes:

1. `cancel_job_tool(..., delete=True)` no longer removes active job records immediately. It cancels the job and returns a message explaining that the record is being retained.
2. `JobManager.update_job()` now treats `CANCELLED` as terminal and ignores late background updates, so cancelled jobs do not get overwritten to `COMPLETED` or accrue new result data after cancellation.

Together, these changes preserve observability and prevent cancelled jobs from being silently rewritten by in-flight background work.

## Does your contribution introduce a new dependency? If yes, which one?

No.

## What should a reviewer concentrate their feedback on?

- Whether `CANCELLED` should be treated as terminal in `update_job()`
- Whether retaining the record for active jobs is the right behavior for `delete=True`
- Whether the tool message is clear enough about why immediate removal is refused

## Did you add any tests for the change?

Yes. The PR adds tests for:

- `cancel_job_tool(job_id, delete=True)` retaining a running job record
- cancelled jobs ignoring late updates from background work

## Any other comments?

Validation run locally:

```
.venv/bin/python -m ruff check src/sktime_mcp/runtime/jobs.py src/sktime_mcp/tools/job_tools.py tests/test_background_jobs.py
.venv/bin/python -m ruff format --check src/sktime_mcp/runtime/jobs.py src/sktime_mcp/tools/job_tools.py tests/test_background_jobs.py
git diff --check
.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_background_jobs.py -q
.venv/bin/python -m pytest -q
```

Full test suite passed locally with the same pre-existing async warnings currently present on `main`.

## Checklist

- [x] Added tests for the change
- [x] Ran local tests
- [x] No new dependency introduced

prs.title: FIX: reject invalid var_name values in export_code_tool
prs.body:
## Reference Issues/PRs

Closes #328.

## What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.

This PR makes `export_code_tool` validate `var_name` before generating Python code.

Before this change, the tool accepted arbitrary strings and interpolated them directly into the generated code. That meant invalid identifiers like:

- `"my model"`
- `"123model"`
- `"class"`

could still return `success: true` while producing non-executable Python.

The new behavior rejects invalid names with:

```python
{"success": False, "error": "var_name must be a valid Python identifier and not a keyword."}
```

This keeps the tool contract honest: a successful export should produce executable code, not syntactically broken output.

## Does your contribution introduce a new dependency? If yes, which one?

No.

## What should a reviewer concentrate their feedback on?

- Whether validating `var_name` at the tool boundary is the right scope
- Whether `str.isidentifier()` plus `keyword.iskeyword()` is sufficient for this use case
- Whether the error message is clear enough for MCP clients and agents to self-correct

## Did you add any tests for the change?

Yes. The PR adds tests covering:

- valid identifier names
- names with spaces
- names starting with digits
- reserved Python keywords
- `export_code_tool` failing fast on invalid `var_name` inputs

## Any other comments?

Validation run locally:

```
.venv/bin/python -m ruff check src/sktime_mcp/tools/codegen.py tests/test_codegen.py
.venv/bin/python -m ruff format --check src/sktime_mcp/tools/codegen.py tests/test_codegen.py
git diff --check

.venv/bin/python -m pytest -q
```

Full test suite passed locally with the same pre-existing async warnings currently present on `main`.

prs.title: FIX: enforce exclusive dataset/data_handle inputs in fit_predict
prs.body:
## Reference Issues/PRs

Closes #324.

## What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.

This PR makes the sync `fit_predict` path reject ambiguous calls that provide both `dataset` and `data_handle`.

Before this change, `fit_predict` silently preferred `data_handle` and ignored `dataset`, while still returning `success: true`. That meant the tool could execute a different request than the one expressed in the arguments.

The async variant already enforces that exactly one of `dataset` or `data_handle` must be provided, so this PR brings the sync path into parity with the existing async contract.

The new behavior is:

- both provided -> `success: false` with `Provide either dataset or data_handle, not both.`
- neither provided -> `success: false` with a clear required-input message
- dataset-only and data_handle-only flows continue to work as before

The validation is implemented in both:

- `fit_predict_tool`
- `Executor.fit_predict`

That keeps the behavior consistent whether the call comes through the tool layer or directly through the executor.

## Does your contribution introduce a new dependency? If yes, which one?

No.

## What should a reviewer concentrate their feedback on?

- Whether the sync error behavior should exactly mirror the async tool wording
- Whether validating at both tool and executor layers is the right consistency boundary
- Whether the neither-provided message is clear enough for MCP clients

## Did you add any tests for the change?

Yes. The PR adds sync coverage for:

- both `dataset` and `data_handle` provided
- neither input provided

It also preserves the existing async parity tests already in the suite.

## Any other comments?

Validation run locally:

```
.venv/bin/python -m ruff check src/sktime_mcp/tools/fit_predict.py src/sktime_mcp/runtime/executor.py tests/test_async_custom_data.py
.venv/bin/python -m ruff format --check src/sktime_mcp/tools/fit_predict.py src/sktime_mcp/runtime/executor.py tests/test_async_custom_data.py
git diff --check

.venv/bin/python -m pytest -q
```

prs.title: FIX: reject invalid cv_folds values in evaluate_estimator_tool
prs.body:
## Reference Issues/PRs

Closes #322.

## What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.

This PR makes `evaluate_estimator_tool` fail fast when `cv_folds` is invalid instead of silently coercing the request to a one-fold evaluation.

Before this change, calls like `cv_folds=0` or `cv_folds=-1` returned `success: true` and ran one fold anyway. That made the response inconsistent with the user request and could mislead MCP clients or LLM agents into believing the requested evaluation actually happened.

The new behavior rejects invalid values with:

```python
{"success": False, "error": "cv_folds must be a positive integer."}
```

Valid inputs still behave the same as before, including clamping large fold counts to the dataset size limit.

## Does your contribution introduce a new dependency? If yes, which one?

No.

## What should a reviewer concentrate their feedback on?

- Whether the validation boundary for `cv_folds` is appropriate
- Whether accepting integer-like string inputs via `int(cv_folds)` is acceptable for tool-level robustness
- Whether the error wording should align with other parameter-validation responses

- `cv_folds=0`

prs.title: FIX: prioritize exact estimator matches in search
prs.body:
## Reference Issues/PRs

Closes #311.

## What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.

This PR updates estimator search ranking so exact estimator class-name matches are returned before broader name, module, or docstring matches.

The change fixes an agent workflow issue observed while testing a basic forecasting workflow: `list_estimators(task="forecasting", query="NaiveForecaster", limit=5)` returned broad forecasting matches before the exact `NaiveForecaster` class, so an agent using a small result page could miss the intended estimator.

The new ranking is:

1. exact case-insensitive estimator name matches
2. estimator names starting with the query
3. estimator names containing the query
4. module-path matches
5. docstring matches

## Does your contribution introduce a new dependency? If yes, which one?

No.

## What should a reviewer concentrate their feedback on?

- Whether the ranking order is appropriate for agent-facing estimator search
- Whether module matches should remain included after name matches
- Whether additional tie-breaking should be considered later

Yes. The PR adds tests for:

- direct registry search prioritizing exact estimator names
- case-insensitive exact-name matching
- `list_estimators(query=..., task=..., limit=...)` keeping the exact estimator first in a small page

prs.title: fix: Avoid panic for unanchorable/non-UTF8 git paths
prs.body:
### What

Prevent a runtime panic in `crates/turborepo-scm/src/git.rs` when git emits paths that cannot be anchored to the turbo root (e.g. corrupted filenames, non-parent paths) or when git stdout contains non-UTF8 bytes. Instead of `unwrap()` → crash, errors propagate through the existing `Result` chain and the change detector falls back to treating all packages as changed.

### Why

GitHub Actions CI was failing for repositories that contain unusual filenames (Cyrillic / corrupted / non-UTF8). The panic originated in `add_files_from_stdout`'s `.unwrap()` calls and caused `--affected` CI runs to crash.

### How

**`crates/turborepo-scm/src/git.rs`**
- `add_files_from_stdout` now returns `Result<(), Error>` — replaced `.unwrap()` on `RelativeUnixPath::new()` and `reanchor_path_from_git_root_to_turbo_root()` with `?` propagation
- All 3 call sites in `changed_files` updated to propagate with `?`

**`crates/turborepo-scope/src/change_detector.rs`**
- Replaced `process::exit(1)` + `eprintln!` with a fail-safe fallback: on any SCM error, log via `tracing::warn!` and return all packages as changed (same pattern as the existing `InvalidRange` handler)
- Removed `std::process` and `Error as ScmError` imports — the library crate no longer terminates the process or couples to specific SCM error variant internals

**`crates/turborepo-repository/src/change_mapper/mod.rs`**
- Added `AllPackageChangeReason::ScmError { error: String }` variant for downstream consumers to understand why all packages were selected

**`crates/turborepo-query/src/lib.rs` + `affected_tasks.rs`**
- Added match arms for the new `ScmError` variant in the GraphQL query layer

### Behavior

When git emits unprocessable paths, instead of panicking:
1. The error propagates from `add_files_from_stdout` through `changed_files` to `changed_packages`
2. `changed_packages` catches it, logs a warning via `tracing::warn!`, and conservatively treats all packages as changed
3. CI runs everything rather than crashing — run summaries, telemetry, and TUI teardown all execute normally

Fix #10403

prs.title: fix: Make selected task highlight use reverse video for accessibility
prs.body:
Fix #10726

This PR improves the visibility of the selected task in the TUI by switching the highlight style from color-only to reverse video `(Modifier::REVERSED)`.

### Why
: The previous approach relied on foreground color, which was difficult to perceive across popular terminal themes and especially for users with color-vision deficiencies. Reverse video provides strong contrast that is theme-agnostic and widely used in terminal UIs for accessibility.

### What changed:

In crates/turborepo-ui/src/tui/table.rs, 
`.highlight_style(Style::default().fg(Color::Yellow))` → 
`.highlight_style(Style::default().add_modifier(Modifier::REVERSED)).`

### Impact:

Visual only; no behavioral changes.

Improves UX for color-blind users (~8% of men, ~0.5% of women) and anyone using low-contrast themes.

prs.title: fix: fully render `.1` release Changelog entries in LTS Changelog archive page
prs.body: #### Fixes #7866 ### Description: - This PR addresses the issue where the [LTS changelog archive](https://www.jenkins.io/changelog-stable-old/) does not fully render changelog entries for `.1` releases. Specifically, entries under `lts_changes` (changes compared to the LTS predecessor) were missing, while only entries under` lts_baseline` (changes compared to the baseline version) were displayed. This fix ensures that both changes and l`ts_changes` are properly rendered for all releases, including `.1` releases. ### Changes Made: - Updated the content/changelog-stable-old/index.html.haml file to include logic for rendering lts_changes alongside changes. - Ensured that the archive page now displays both changes and lts_changes for each release, similar to the main changelog page (changelog-stable). - Verified the fix by testing the archive page with multiple .1 releases to confirm that all entries are now fully rendered. ### Testing: - ran `make` and `make run` to view my changes - Tested the changes locally by rendering the archive page and verifying that .1 releases (e.g., 2.375.1) now display all expected changelog entries. ## Example Screenshots - ### Before : ### After : CC: @kmartens27

prs.title: Add support for custom Maven properties in Matrix build
prs.body:
#### Fixes https://github.com/jenkins-infra/pipeline-library/issues/851

### Description
This PR adds support for custom Maven properties in the Matrix build configuration for the `winp` repository. The changes allow users to specify different Maven properties (e.g., `native.configuration=Release` and `native.configuration=Debug`) for parallel builds.

### Changes
1. **Matrix Build Configuration**:
   - Added a `matrix` block to define multiple axes for the build.
   - Introduced a `NATIVE_CONFIG` axis with values `Release` and `Debug`.
   - Retained the existing `JDK` axis for JDK versions 21 and 17.

2. **Custom Maven Properties**:
   - Added a `mavenProps` variable to pass custom Maven properties (`-Dnative.configuration=${NATIVE_CONFIG}`) to the `mvn clean install` command.

3. **Agent Configuration**:
   - Updated the `agent` block to dynamically select the platform (`linux` or `windows`) based on the `PLATFORM` variable.

prs.title: feat: add "Report Infra Issue" link to `/download` and `/download/mirrors` pages footer
prs.body:
#### Fixes https://github.com/jenkins-infra/jenkins.io/issues/5269.

### Description


- This PR adds a "Report Infra Issue" link to the footer of the /download and /download/mirrors pages. The link points to the Jenkins Infra Helpdesk issues page (https://github.com/jenkins-infra/helpdesk/issues), allowing users to report infrastructure-related issues directly from these pages.

- This change addresses the issue where users were incorrectly using the "Report a Problem" link to report infrastructure-related issues, which should instead be directed to the Jenkins Infra Helpdesk.

### Changes 

1. New Component:

   - Added a new jio-report-infra-issue component that renders a link to the Jenkins Infra Helpdesk issues page.

2. Conditional Rendering:

   - Updated jio-footer.ts to conditionally render the jio-report-infra-issue component on the /download and /download/mirrors pages.

   - Other pages continue to display the existing "Report a Problem" link.

3. Styling:

   - Updated jio-footer.css to ensure the new link matches the existing footer styling.

4. Storybook Tests:

   - Added ReportInfraIssue.stories.ts to test the new component's behavior, including cases where required props are missing.

5. Bug Fixes:

   - Fixed an issue where the jio-report-infra-issue component was rendering a link even when githubRepo was not provided. -- The component now only renders the link when githubRepo is present.

prs.title: feat: Convert latitude/longitude fields to GeoJSON for map data
prs.body:
### Fixes #5 

## Description : 

- This PR introduces the following changes to improve the handling of map data in the user stories.

## Changes Made : 

1. **Replaced latitude and longitude fields with a `geojson` field**:

- The `geojson` field now stores spatial data as a GeoJSON string, aligning with Decap CMS's map widget requirements.
- This eliminates redundancy and ensures consistency in how map data is stored and used.

2. **Updated `config.yml`**:

- Removed the latitude and longitude fields from the Decap CMS admin interface.
- Added a `geojson` field with the map widget, allowing users to select a point on the map directly.

3. **Modified `map.jsx`**:

- Updated the GraphQL query to fetch the `geojson` field instead of latitude and longitude.
- Parsed the `geojson` string to extract coordinates and render map markers.

4. **Script to Migrate Existing Data**:

- Added a script (`convertToGeoJSON.js`) to migrate existing latitude and longitude values to the geojson field in all index.yaml files.


## Impact : 

1. **Simplified Data Structure**: The `geojson` field serves as a single source of truth for map data, reducing redundancy and potential inconsistencies.

2. **Alignment with Decap CMS**: The changes align with Decap CMS's map widget, which expects spatial data in GeoJSON format.

3. **Improved User Experience**: Users can now select a point on the map directly in the admin interface, making it easier to input and manage map data.


## Testing
- Verified that the map markers render correctly on the user stories map page.
- Confirmed backward compatibility by ensuring existing `latitude` and `longitude` values were successfully migrated to `geojson`.

Please review it and let me know for improvements.

issues.title: [DOC/TEST] add external agent integration guide and runnable workflow example
issues.body:
## Summary
There is currently no single place that shows how to integrate `sktime-mcp` from an external agent codebase while also clarifying the current evaluation-tool contract.

## Why this matters
Recent real-world agent integration feedback shows two recurring friction points:

1. Local integration setup is still easy to get wrong when people import from source trees directly.
2. `evaluate_estimator` is powerful, but agents benefit from a concrete example of the currently supported inputs and outputs.

## Proposal
Add:
- a short user-guide section for external agent integration (`pip install -e .`, `PYTHONPATH` fallback)
- a runnable example that demonstrates:
  - loading custom data
  - reading returned metadata
  - fitting/predicting from a `data_handle`
  - evaluating on a demo dataset with `cv_folds`
- a smoke test so this workflow stays trustworthy over time

## Expected benefit
This would make `sktime-mcp` easier to adopt from custom agent projects and would give contributors a concrete, tested workflow artifact to point to.

issues.title: [ENH] expose explicit data shape metadata for agent reasoning
issues.body:
## Summary
`load_data_source` and `load_data_source_async` currently return basic metadata such as rows, columns, dates, and dtypes, but they do not explicitly tell an agent whether a loaded handle is univariate or multivariate, whether exogenous variables are present, or what kind of index backs the series.

## Why this matters
Agentic workflows often need to decide which estimator family or tool path to take before fitting. Right now an agent has to infer too much from raw column names and dtypes.

Concrete gaps:
- univariate vs multivariate target is not explicit
- exogenous presence is not explicit
- exogenous width is not explicit
- index type is not explicit (`datetime` vs `period` vs `range` / `integer`)

This makes tool selection and model routing harder than it needs to be.

## Proposal
Augment data-handle metadata with explicit agent-friendly fields, for example:
- `target_scitype`
- `target_variates`
- `has_exog`
- `exog_variates`
- `index_type`
- `n_target_columns`
- `n_exog_columns`

## Expected benefit
This would make loaded data self-describing for LLM / MCP agents and reduce avoidable reasoning errors when selecting estimators or deciding whether exogenous-aware workflows are available.

issues.title: [DOC/TEST] add runnable agentic model-selection workflow example using existing MCP tools
issues.body:
## Summary
The repository already has solid MCP primitives for discovery, instantiation, evaluation, forecasting, and code export, but it does not currently ship a runnable example that ties those together into a simple agent-style model-selection loop.

## Why this matters
One of the clearest ways to demonstrate the value of `sktime-mcp` for agentic workflows is a truthful end-to-end example where an LLM-style loop:
- discovers candidate forecasters
- evaluates them on a dataset
- picks a winner based on a metric
- generates a forecast
- exports reproducible code

This is directly aligned with the project's agentic positioning and gives contributors/users a concrete workflow artifact to run and inspect.

## Proposed addition
Add a new example script and smoke test covering a minimal model-selection loop on a built-in dataset using the existing MCP tool surface, without introducing any new framework or tool abstraction.

## Suggested scope
- new runnable example in `examples/`
- smoke test in `tests/`
- use current tools only: `list_estimators`, `describe_estimator`, `instantiate_estimator`, `evaluate_estimator`, `fit_predict`, `export_code`

issues.title: [BUG] own-data examples are not smoke-tested and SQL example fails noisily without optional dependencies
issues.body:
## Summary
The repository includes runnable examples for MCP workflows on user-provided data (`pandas`, `csv`, `sql`), but they are not covered by tests. As a result, the SQL example currently fails noisily in a standard dev environment when the optional SQL dependency is not installed.

## Reproduction
Run:
```bash
python examples/sql_example.py
```

On an environment without `sqlalchemy`, the script currently attempts the runnable SQLite workflow anyway, which triggers executor error logging and stack traces before the example reaches the template-only sections.

## Why this matters
The E2E examples are part of the first real MCP workflows users try when bringing their own data. For those workflows to be trustworthy:
- runnable examples should be smoke-tested
- optional-dependency examples should degrade clearly and intentionally
- example output should not look like the MCP stack is broken when the real issue is just a missing extra

## Expected behavior
- `pandas`, `csv`, and pipeline examples should be exercised by lightweight smoke tests
- `sql_example.py` should either run successfully when SQL support is installed, or print a clean install/skip message when it is not
- no noisy traceback-style example output for an expected optional-dependency case

issues.title: [BUG] agentic workflow examples drift from current MCP behavior and are not smoke-tested
issues.body:
## Summary
The repository ships several agentic / MCP workflow examples, but they are not currently exercised by tests. As a result, some example narratives have drifted from the current runtime behavior.

## Reproduced drift
- `examples/02_llm_query_simulation.py` compares models on `sunspots`, but that dataset is not in the currently exposed demo dataset list, so the simulated `fit_predict` calls return `success: false`.
- The same example uses `Detrend` instead of the actual estimator name `Detrender`, producing an `Unknown estimator` validation result.
- `examples/01_forecasting_workflow.py` uses a forecaster→forecaster invalid-composition demonstration that no longer reflects current validator behavior on `main`.

## Why this matters
These example scripts are exactly the kind of workflow artifacts an LLM/agent user will read and run first. If they drift from the runtime, they become misleading documentation for agentic MCP usage.

## Expected behavior
- documented agentic/MCP examples should use dataset names and estimator names that work on current `main`
- high-level example scripts should be covered by lightweight smoke tests so drift is caught in CI before release

issues.title: [BUG] auto_format_on_load crashes internally for valid RangeIndex time series
issues.body:
## Summary
When `load_data_source` receives valid integer-indexed pandas data with auto-format enabled, the request succeeds but the auto-format step logs an internal failure:

```
Auto-formatting failed: 'RangeIndex' object has no attribute 'freq'
```

## Reproduction
```python
from sktime_mcp.runtime.executor import Executor

Executor().load_data_source({
    "type": "pandas",
    "data": {"y": [1, 2, 3, 4]},
})
```

## Current behavior
- returns `success=True`
- auto-formatting falls back after logging an internal failure
- valid integer-indexed time series do not go through the normal formatting path

## Expected behavior
- integer / `RangeIndex` inputs should be treated as valid forecasting data
- auto-formatting should skip datetime-only frequency inference for non-datetime indexes
- metadata should preserve a sensible frequency label such as `Integer` instead of degrading or failing

## Why this matters
The adapters and validation layer already allow integer-style indexes. The formatter should not assume datetime-only frequency metadata, otherwise a valid workflow partially fails behind the scenes and becomes harder for MCP clients to trust.

issues.title: [BUG] export_code accepts unsupported demo datasets for fit examples and generates misleading forecasting code
issues.body:
## Summary
`export_code_tool(..., include_fit_example=True, dataset=...)` currently accepts demo datasets that are not compatible with the forecasting-oriented fit example template.

## Reproduction
On current `main`, this succeeds:

```python
export_code_tool(handle, include_fit_example=True, dataset="basic_motions")
```

and generates code like:

```python
from sktime.datasets import load_basic_motions
y = load_basic_motions()
model.fit(y)
```

## Why it matters
`basic_motions` is not a forecasting demo dataset in the current high-level workflow. Generated code is trusted output, so producing misleading example code is worse than rejecting the request.

## Suggested fix
When `include_fit_example=True`, validate that the requested dataset is compatible with the forecasting-oriented fit example template and return a clear error for unsupported demo datasets.

issues.title: [BUG] demo dataset discovery advertises non-forecasting datasets that high-level workflows cannot use
issues.body:
## Summary
The runtime currently auto-discovers all `sktime.datasets.load_*` functions and advertises them through demo dataset discovery.

After rejecting tuple-based supervised datasets in `load_dataset`, discovery still lists non-forecasting datasets like `basic_motions`, `arrow_head`, and `gunpoint` as available demos.

## Reproduction
On current code, `list_available_data_tool(is_demo=True)` includes datasets such as:
- `basic_motions`
- `arrow_head`
- `gunpoint`

But those datasets are not actually usable in the current forecasting-oriented high-level workflow.

## Why it matters
This leaves the MCP surface misleading at the discovery layer. Agents are told these datasets are valid demo choices, only to hit a failure later when they try to load them.

## Suggested fix
Filter discovery so only demo datasets compatible with the current forecasting-oriented workflow are advertised.

issues.title: [BUG] load_dataset misinterprets tuple-based non-forecasting demo datasets as forecasting y/exog
issues.body:
## Summary
`Executor.load_dataset()` currently assumes tuple-returning demo loaders use forecasting semantics `(y, X)`.

Because demo datasets are auto-discovered from all `sktime.datasets.load_*` functions, classification datasets like `basic_motions`, `arrow_head`, and `gunpoint` are also exposed. Those loaders return supervised-learning style tuples where features come first and class labels come second.

## Reproduction
On current `main`, these datasets appear in the discovered demo dataset map and can be loaded through `Executor.load_dataset()`.

For example, `basic_motions` currently returns:
- `data`: feature `DataFrame`
- `exog`: class-label `ndarray`

That means labels are surfaced as forecasting exogenous variables.

## Why it matters
This is a core workflow correctness bug. MCP clients can see these datasets as available demos, but the runtime silently interprets them with forecasting semantics, which is misleading for downstream tool calls.

## Suggested fix
Fail fast for tuple-based supervised datasets that are not forecasting-compatible, with a clear error explaining that the dataset is not supported by the current forecasting-oriented high-level workflows.

issues.title: [BUG] invalid numeric server env vars crash MCP startup at import time
issues.body:
## Summary
`src/sktime_mcp/server.py` currently parses `SKTIME_MCP_JOB_MAX_AGE_HOURS` and `SKTIME_MCP_JOB_CLEANUP_INTERVAL` with bare `int(...)` calls at import time.

## Reproduction
Examples reproduced on current `main`:

```bash
SKTIME_MCP_JOB_MAX_AGE_HOURS=abc
```

or

```bash
SKTIME_MCP_JOB_CLEANUP_INTERVAL=abc
```

Then importing `sktime_mcp.server` crashes immediately with:

```python
ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'abc'
```

## Why it matters
A misconfigured environment variable should not prevent the MCP server from starting at all. This affects server availability before any tool can be called.

## Suggested fix
Parse numeric env vars through a small helper that falls back to defaults and logs a warning instead of raising during import.

issues.title: [BUG] list_available_data_tool accepts invalid is_demo values and returns misleading empty results
issues.body:
## Summary
`list_available_data_tool` currently accepts non-boolean values for `is_demo` and returns `success=True` with empty results instead of a validation error.

## Reproduction
Examples reproduced on current `main`:

```python
list_available_data_tool("false")
# {'success': True, 'system_demos': [], 'active_handles': [], 'total': 0}
```

```python
list_available_data_tool(1)
# {'success': True, 'system_demos': [], 'active_handles': [], 'total': 0}
```

```python
list_available_data_tool([])
# {'success': True, 'system_demos': [], 'active_handles': [], 'total': 0}
```

## Why it matters
This is an MCP-facing reliability issue. Tool callers and LLM agents can pass malformed boolean-like values, and the current behavior implies that no data exists instead of surfacing invalid input.

## Suggested fix
Require `is_demo` to be `True`, `False`, or `None`, and return a structured `success=False` error for any other type.

issues.title: [BUG] format_time_series_tool accepts non-boolean control flags and mutates data unexpectedly
issues.body:
## Summary
`format_time_series_tool` currently accepts non-boolean values for `auto_infer_freq`, `fill_missing`, and `remove_duplicates`. Because the tool relies on Python truthiness, malformed values like strings can silently enable mutations that the caller did not request.

## Reproduction
Examples reproduced on current `main`:

```python
format_time_series_tool(..., remove_duplicates="false", fill_missing=False, auto_infer_freq=False)
# success=True
# duplicates are still removed
```

```python
format_time_series_tool(..., remove_duplicates=False, fill_missing="false", auto_infer_freq=False)
# success=True
# missing values are still filled
```

```python
format_time_series_tool(..., auto_infer_freq="false")
# truthy string sends the tool down frequency inference paths
```

## Why it matters
This is an MCP-facing reliability issue. Tool callers and LLM agents can pass stringified booleans, and the current behavior may silently rewrite user data instead of returning a validation error.

## Suggested fix
Validate that `auto_infer_freq`, `fill_missing`, and `remove_duplicates` are real booleans and return structured `success=False` errors for invalid types.

A small sibling fix may also make sense for `auto_format_on_load_tool(enabled=...)`, which currently accepts values like `"false"` and reports confusing success messages.

issues.title: [BUG] cancel_job_tool treats non-boolean delete values as truthy and removes jobs unexpectedly
issues.body:
## Summary
`cancel_job_tool` does not validate the `delete` parameter type. Because Python treats non-empty strings as truthy, malformed MCP calls like `delete="false"` can still delete job records.

## Reproduced on current code (April 29, 2026)
```python
from sktime_mcp.runtime.jobs import get_job_manager, JobStatus
from sktime_mcp.tools.job_tools import cancel_job_tool

jm = get_job_manager()
job_id = jm.create_job("fit_predict", "handle", "ARIMA")
jm.update_job(job_id, status=JobStatus.COMPLETED)

cancel_job_tool(job_id, delete="false")
# -> {"success": True, "message": "Job '...' removed"}
```

The job record is removed even though the caller clearly did not provide a boolean.

## Why this matters
For MCP/JSON tools, `delete` should be a strict boolean. Silently treating strings or integers as truthy can cause destructive behavior from malformed tool calls.

## Expected behavior
`cancel_job_tool` should reject non-boolean `delete` values with a validation error instead of deleting anything.

## Suggested fix
Add a strict `isinstance(delete, bool)` guard near the start of `cancel_job_tool`, plus regression tests for string/integer inputs.

issues.title: [BUG] discovery and job listing tools accept boolean pagination values
issues.body:
## Summary
Several MCP tools validate `limit`/`offset` only with numeric comparisons like `limit < 1`, which allows Python boolean values through because `bool` is a subclass of `int`.

## Reproduced on current main (April 29, 2026)
```python
search_estimators_tool("NaiveForecaster", limit=True)
# success=True, behaves like limit=1

list_estimators_tool(limit=True)
# success=True, returns one estimator and echoes "limit": True

list_estimators_tool(limit=2, offset=True)
# success=True, behaves like offset=1

list_jobs_tool(limit=True)
# success=True
```

## Why this matters
For MCP/JSON tools, `true`/`false` should not silently become pagination values. In agent-driven workflows this masks type mistakes and makes the contract less reliable.

## Affected tools
- `search_estimators_tool`
- `list_estimators_tool`
- `list_jobs_tool`

## Expected behavior
These tools should reject boolean values for pagination parameters and return the same style of validation error used for other invalid numeric inputs.

## Suggested fix
Use strict integer validation for pagination parameters, for example rejecting `bool` explicitly before the existing range checks.

issues.title: [BUG] forecasting tools accept boolean horizon values and coerce True to 1
issues.body:
#### What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.

`fit_predict_tool` and `fit_predict_async_tool` currently accept boolean values for `horizon` because the validation uses `isinstance(horizon, int)`, and in Python `bool` is a subclass of `int`.

That means:

```python
fit_predict_tool(handle, "airline", horizon=True)
```

returns success and behaves like `horizon=1`, while:

```python
fit_predict_async_tool(handle, dataset="airline", horizon=True)
```

starts a background job with `horizon: True`.

#### Why this matters

For an MCP tool, `true`/`false` in JSON should not silently become forecast horizons. This is especially risky for LLM-driven tool use, where type slips can happen and a successful response hides the problem.

#### Steps to reproduce

```python
from sktime_mcp.tools.instantiate import instantiate_estimator_tool
from sktime_mcp.tools.fit_predict import fit_predict_tool

result = instantiate_estimator_tool("NaiveForecaster")
handle = result["handle"]

fit_predict_tool(handle, "airline", horizon=True)
# currently succeeds and returns a 1-step forecast
```

#### Expected behavior

All forecasting tool entrypoints should reject boolean horizons with the same validation error used for other non-integer values.

#### Proposed fix

Treat `bool` as invalid in the shared horizon validator, and add regression tests for:
- `predict_tool(..., horizon=True/False)`
- `fit_predict_tool(..., horizon=True)`
- `fit_predict_async_tool(..., horizon=True)`

issues.title: [BUG] estimator discovery tools crash or mis-handle invalid top-level input types
issues.body:
## Summary
Several core estimator discovery tools do not validate their top-level inputs before using them. This leads to Python exceptions or misleading responses instead of structured MCP errors.

## Reproduction
Using current `main`:
```python
from sktime_mcp.tools.describe_estimator import describe_estimator_tool
from sktime_mcp.tools.list_estimators import list_estimators_tool

describe_estimator_tool(["ARIMA"])
# TypeError: unhashable type: list

describe_estimator_tool(123)
# AttributeError: int object has no attribute lower

list_estimators_tool(task=["forecasting"])
# success=True, count=0

list_estimators_tool(tags=["capability:pred_int"])
# error from internals: list object has no attribute items

list_estimators_tool(query=["ARIMA"])
# error from internals: list object has no attribute lower
```

## Expected behavior
These tools should reject malformed top-level inputs with `success: false` and a clear validation error.

## Why this matters
These are primary MCP discovery entrypoints for LLM agents. Crashes and misleading empty results make planning and self-correction much harder than structured tool-level validation errors.

## Proposed fix
- validate that `describe_estimator_tool` receives a non-empty string estimator name
- validate that `search_estimators_tool` receives a non-empty string query
- validate that `list_estimators_tool` receives `task: str | None`, `tags: dict | None`, and `query: str | None`
- add focused tests for malformed top-level inputs

issues.title: [BUG] instantiation tools crash or mis-handle invalid top-level argument types
issues.body:
## Summary
The instantiation tools validate `params`, but do not validate the top-level `estimator` and `components` arguments before using them. That leads to Python exceptions or misleading behavior instead of structured MCP errors.

## Reproduction
Using current `main`:
```python
from sktime_mcp.tools.instantiate import instantiate_estimator_tool, instantiate_pipeline_tool

instantiate_estimator_tool(["ARIMA"])
# TypeError: unhashable type: list

instantiate_pipeline_tool("ARIMA")
# treated as ["A", "R", "I", "M", "A"] and returns misleading validation errors

instantiate_pipeline_tool(123)
# TypeError: object of type int has no len()
```

## Expected behavior
- `instantiate_estimator_tool` should reject non-string estimator names with `success: false`
- `instantiate_pipeline_tool` should require a non-empty list of strings and reject malformed inputs with `success: false`

## Why this matters
These are core MCP entrypoints. LLM clients can send malformed top-level argument types, and tool-level crashes are much harder to recover from than structured validation errors.

## Proposed fix
- validate that `estimator` is a non-empty string
- validate that `components` is a non-empty list of non-empty strings
- add focused tests for malformed top-level inputs

issues.title: [BUG] list_jobs_tool crashes on non-string status values instead of returning validation error
issues.body:
## Summary
`list_jobs_tool` validates invalid status strings, but crashes with `AttributeError` when `status` is a non-string value like `true`, `1`, or `["running"]`.

## Reproduction
Using current `main`:
```python
from sktime_mcp.tools.job_tools import list_jobs_tool

list_jobs_tool(status=True)
# AttributeError: bool object has no attribute lower

list_jobs_tool(status=1)
# AttributeError: int object has no attribute lower
```

## Expected behavior
The tool should return a structured MCP error response instead of raising an exception, similar to the existing invalid-string path.

## Why this matters
This is an LLM-facing reliability bug. MCP clients can send malformed argument types, and a tool-level crash is much harder for agents to recover from than a normal validation error.

## Proposed fix
- validate that `status` is a string before calling `.lower()`
- keep the existing invalid-value error for unknown status strings
- add focused tests for non-string inputs

issues.title: [BUG] export_code_tool accepts invalid var_name values and generates non-executable Python
issues.body:
## Problem

`export_code_tool` currently accepts any string for `var_name` and interpolates it directly into generated Python code. Invalid identifiers such as names with spaces, names starting with digits, or Python keywords still return `success: true`, but the exported code is not executable Python.

## Reproduction

```python
from sktime_mcp.tools.instantiate import instantiate_estimator_tool
from sktime_mcp.tools.codegen import export_code_tool

inst = instantiate_estimator_tool("NaiveForecaster", {"strategy": "last"})
handle = inst["handle"]

print(export_code_tool(handle, var_name="my model")["code"])
print(export_code_tool(handle, var_name="123model")["code"])
print(export_code_tool(handle, var_name="class")["code"])
```

Current behavior examples:

```python
my model = NaiveForecaster(strategy="last")
123model = NaiveForecaster(strategy="last")
class = NaiveForecaster(strategy="last")
```

All of these responses are returned with `success: true` even though the generated code is invalid Python.

## Expected behavior

`export_code_tool` should fail fast when `var_name` is not a valid Python identifier, for example:

```python
{"success": False, "error": "var_name must be a valid Python identifier and not a keyword."}
```

## Why this matters

`export_code_tool` is meant to produce executable code that an MCP client or user can trust. Returning syntactically invalid code with a success response breaks that contract and can derail agent workflows that try to run or save the generated output directly.

## Suggested fix

- validate `var_name` before code generation
- require `str.isidentifier()` to pass
- reject Python keywords via `keyword.iskeyword()`
- add tests covering valid names, names with spaces, names starting with digits, and reserved keywords

issues.title: [BUG] fit_predict silently ignores dataset when data_handle is also provided
issues.body:
## Problem

`fit_predict` is documented as accepting a demo `dataset` or a custom `data_handle`, but the sync tool does not reject calls where both are provided. In that case it silently uses `data_handle` and ignores `dataset`, while still returning `success: true`.

This creates a contract mismatch with `fit_predict_async`, which already enforces that exactly one of `dataset` or `data_handle` must be provided.

## Reproduction

```python
from sktime_mcp.tools.instantiate import instantiate_estimator_tool
from sktime_mcp.tools.fit_predict import fit_predict_tool
from sktime_mcp.runtime.executor import get_executor
import pandas as pd

inst = instantiate_estimator_tool("NaiveForecaster", {"strategy": "last"})
handle = inst["handle"]

executor = get_executor()
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": pd.date_range("2020-01-01", periods=12, freq="ME"),
    "value": range(12),
})
load = executor.load_data_source({
    "type": "pandas",
    "data": df,
    "time_column": "date",
    "target_column": "value",
})

result = fit_predict_tool(
    estimator_handle=handle,
    dataset="airline",
    data_handle=load["data_handle"],
    horizon=3,
)
print(result["success"])
```

Current behavior:

- returns `success: true`
- silently uses the custom `data_handle` path
- ignores the `dataset` argument without warning

## Expected behavior

Calls that provide both inputs should fail fast with a clear validation error, for example:

```python
{"success": False, "error": "Provide either dataset or data_handle, not both."}\n```\n\n## Why this matters\n\nThis is an MCP agent-trust issue. If the caller supplies both values accidentally, the current behavior executes a different request than the one expressed in the arguments, and the success response gives no signal that one input was ignored.\n\n## Suggested fix\n\n- add explicit validation in `fit_predict_tool`\n- add the same validation in `Executor.fit_predict` for consistency at the execution layer\n- add tests for both-provided and neither-provided cases\n- keep `fit_predict` behavior aligned with `fit_predict_async`\n

issues.title: [BUG] evaluate_estimator_tool silently coerces zero or negative cv_folds to 1
issues.body:
## Problem

`evaluate_estimator_tool` currently accepts `cv_folds=0` and negative values, but instead of rejecting them it silently coerces them to a single-fold evaluation and returns `success: true`. For MCP/LLM workflows, this is misleading because the caller asked for an invalid number of folds and receives a valid-looking result.

## Reproduction

```python
from sktime_mcp.tools.instantiate import instantiate_estimator_tool
from sktime_mcp.tools.evaluate import evaluate_estimator_tool

inst = instantiate_estimator_tool("NaiveForecaster", {"strategy": "last"})
handle = inst["handle"]

print(evaluate_estimator_tool(handle, "airline", 0))
print(evaluate_estimator_tool(handle, "airline", -1))
```

Observed behavior:

- `cv_folds=0` returns `success: true` with `cv_folds_run: 1`
- `cv_folds=-1` returns `success: true` with `cv_folds_run: 1`

The response still reports the original invalid request in `cv_folds_requested`, which makes the result internally inconsistent.

## Root cause

In `src/sktime_mcp/tools/evaluate.py`, the tool currently does:

```python
folds = max(1, min(int(cv_folds), max(1, n - 1)))
```

This clamps invalid non-positive inputs up to `1` instead of rejecting them.

## Expected behavior

Invalid fold counts should fail fast with a clear validation error, for example:

```python
{"success": False, "error": "cv_folds must be a positive integer."}
```

## Why this matters

This is an agent-trust issue. An LLM can incorrectly believe it successfully ran 0-fold or negative-fold cross-validation, when the tool actually executed a different evaluation than requested. Explicit validation would make failures easier to recover from and keep evaluation outputs aligned with user intent.

## Suggested fix

Before computing the splitter:

- validate that `cv_folds` is an integer greater than 0
- return a clear error for `0`, negative values, or non-integer-like inputs
- add tests covering these invalid cases

issues.title: [BUG] list_estimators silently returns empty results for invalid task/tag filters
issues.body:
## Problem

`list_estimators_tool` currently returns `success: true` with zero results when callers pass an invalid task name or an invalid capability tag key. For an MCP/LLM workflow, this is misleading: the agent may interpret the response as "no suitable estimators exist" instead of "the filter was invalid."

This was also observed in early adopter testing feedback in #35: https://github.com/sktime/sktime-mcp/issues/35#issuecomment-4209342028

## Reproduction

```python
from sktime_mcp.tools.list_estimators import list_estimators_tool

print(list_estimators_tool(task="forcasting", limit=5))
print(list_estimators_tool(task="forecasting", tags={"pred_int": True}, limit=5))
```

Current behavior:

```python
{"success": True, "count": 0, "total": 0, ...}
{"success": True, "count": 0, "total": 0, ...}
```

## Expected behavior

Invalid filters should be reported explicitly instead of silently returning an empty successful result. For example:

- invalid `task="forcasting"` should return `success: false` with valid task options and ideally a suggestion such as `forecasting`
- invalid tag key `pred_int` should return `success: false` with guidance to use a valid tag such as `capability:pred_int`

## Why this matters

`list_estimators` is one of the core planning tools for agent-driven workflows. Silent empty results can derail downstream model selection because the LLM has no signal that its query was malformed. Explicit validation would make the tool easier for agents to recover from and would reduce false negatives during estimator discovery.

## Suggested fix

Add lightweight validation before filtering:

- validate `task` against the registry task list
- validate requested tag keys against available registry tags
- return a clear error message with valid options and/or close-match suggestions
- add tests for invalid task names and invalid tag keys

issues.title: [BUG] list_estimators query does not prioritize exact estimator name matches
issues.body:
## Summary

`list_estimators(query=...)` can return broad name/docstring matches before an exact estimator class-name match. During an agent workflow test, `list_estimators(task="forecasting", query="NaiveForecaster", limit=5)` returned other forecasting estimators before the exact `NaiveForecaster` class.

## Why this matters

Agents often search by a known estimator name before instantiating it. If exact class-name matches are not ranked first, small result limits can hide the intended estimator and make the workflow less reliable.

## Suggested fix

Rank search results so that:

1. exact case-insensitive estimator name matches come first,
2. prefix/name substring matches come next,
3. docstring/module matches come after name matches.

Add tests covering exact-name and case-insensitive queries.

issues.title: [ENH] Expose available task discovery as an MCP tool
issues.body:
## Summary

`sktime-mcp` already has a `get_available_tasks()` helper, but it is not exposed through the MCP tool list. Agents can discover queryable tags with `get_available_tags`, but cannot ask the server which task filters are currently available before calling `list_estimators`.

## Why this matters

Task discovery is useful for agent-driven workflows because the valid `list_estimators(task=...)` values can change as the registry expands. Exposing this as a tool lets clients avoid guessing task names and makes workflows more robust when new task families are added.

## Suggested change

- Register a `get_available_tasks` MCP tool.
- Dispatch it to the existing `get_available_tasks()` helper.
- Add tests for tool registration and successful calls.
- Add a small documentation mention alongside `get_available_tags`.

issues.title: Evaluate  "Upgrade to Java 11" documentation in light of Jenkins Java 17+ requirement

labels.name: documentation
labels.description: Jenkins documentation, including user and developer docs, solution pages, etc.

issues.body:
### Describe your use-case which is not covered by existing documentation.

The current [Upgrade to Java 11](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/platform-information/upgrade-java-to-11/index.html) documentation page may be outdated since Jenkins now requires Java 17 or higher. This page could potentially mislead users into thinking that upgrading to Java 11 is still a valid or supported target.

We need to discuss whether to:

1. *Remove the Java 11 upgrade page entirely*

2. *Archive it for legacy reference*

3. *Redirect it to the Java 17 (or later) upgrade documentation*

4. *Or keep it as is for users currently on older Java versions (e.g., Java 8) who might find it useful on their upgrade path*

Input from the community would help decide the best approach to maintain clarity in our docs.
Keeping this as a discussion to gather broader feedback.

### Reference any relevant documentation, other materials or issues/pull requests that can be used for inspiration.

[Upgrade to Java 11](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/platform-information/upgrade-java-to-11/index.html)

Yes, but after having a discussion with community members.
